headline: Washington's ground game suffocates UCLA in defensive showcase
body: Washington's defense made life miserable for UCLA's passing attack, and the Huskies' ground game did the rest. Isaiah Ward dominated the edge for Washington, but the real story was simpler: 199 rushing yards to 23 forced UCLA into obvious passing situations all night. Iamaleava threw for 293 yards and a touchdown, but volume without efficiency rarely translates to points. He was sacked once and his defense gave him almost no help up front.

The Bruins' offense looked fragmented despite Iamaleava's arm talent. Rowe was their lifeline through the air with 106 yards on six catches, yet UCLA managed just seven points across sixty minutes. The fumble on Washington's side of the field never materialized into points. What should have been a manageable game became a grind the moment UCLA fell behind and couldn't establish any semblance of a run game. Thomas carried just six times for 27 yards—the coaching room appeared to abandon the ground attack early.

Washington's QB Williams Jr. didn't need to be spectacular. He managed 169 yards on efficient passing while the running back Washington chewed up yards methodically, scoring once. That's the blueprint when your defense is playing this well. The Huskies held UCLA to field goal range territory and made sure the Bruins paid the price for every mistake. This was defense-first football executed cleanly, and UCLA simply couldn't answer the bell.
turningPoint: UCLA's three-and-out on their opening drive set the tone—Washington's defense established control immediately, and once the Bruins fell behind, they were forced into one-dimensional passing that Ward and the pass rush readily exploited.

unsungHero.name: Manu
unsungHero.why: Two sacks in a game where the defense was clearly the story, yet Ward got the hardware. Manu's interior pressure forced Iamaleava into hurried decisions all night.
